source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Consider a $2-\text{cm}$ thick plastic scintillator directly coupled to the surface of a photomultiplier with a gain of $10^{6}$. A $10-\text{GeV}$ particle beam is incident on the scintillator as shown in Fig. 4.8(a). If the beam particle is a muon, estimate the charge collected at the anode of the photomultiplier.

multi_step: question asks to estimate an anode charge from a particle/scintillator/photomultiplier setup rather than retrieve a single fact
question_text includes several physical conditions to combine: scintillator thickness, particle type/energy, and photomultiplier gain
answer is a computed numeric quantity, which suggests multi-step physics reasoning or calculation
verifiability: answer_type is short_text with a specific numeric result: 3.2 x 10^-10 C
the target output is a single physical quantity with units, which is straightforward to compare against expected magnitude/form

candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: Consider a $2-\text{cm}$ thick plastic scintillator directly coupled to the surface of a photomultiplier with a gain of $10^{6}$. A $10-\text{GeV}$ particle beam is incident on the scintillator as shown in Fig. 4.8(a). If the beam particle is a muon, estimate the charge collected at the anode of the photomultiplier.
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: images field contains a PIL image placeholder string rather than a concrete file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: From its ionization loss curve, we see that a muon of energy 10 GeV will lose 4 MeV in a plastic scintillator of length 2 cm . Roughly, in a plastic scintillator, producing one photon requires 100 eV of energy. This amount of energy will produce $N_{\text{ph}} \approx 4 \times 10^{4}$ photons in the scintillator. Suppose about $50 %$ of the photons make it to the photomultiplier tube and about $10 %$ of these produce photoelectrons off the cathode. Then the number of photoelectrons emitted is $N_{\text{pe}}=2 \times 10^{3}$. With a gain of $10^{6}$, the charge collected at the anode of the photomultiplier is $Q=2 \times 10^{9} e=3.2 \times 10^{-10} \text{C}$
